result:
Both, different jobs.

Consumption path check: gate every redeem on expired_at > now(). Enforces correctness at point of use. Without it, batch job lag lets an expired card redeem.

Pattern: add AND expired_at > now() to the SELECT FOR UPDATE. If no row, distinguish "not found" from "expired" for UX — do a second read without the predicate to decide the error message.

Batch job check: nightly sweep marks expired cards (status = 'expired'), writes audit event, emits notification 30/7/1 days before expiry.
Purpose: not correctness, but side effects consumption path can't do — emails, analytics rollups, escheatment reporting, clearing balance for accounting, freeing reserved float on the balance sheet.

Why both: consumption path is authoritative ("can I spend this right now"), batch is operational ("tell the world this card died, update reports, notify user"). Neither replaces the other.

Third thing worth adding: expired_at column indexed, status enum (active/expired/disabled/fully_redeemed). Consumption checks status='active' AND expired_at > now(). Batch job flips status. Makes admin queries and reporting cheap — don't recompute expiry everywhere.

Timezone: store expired_at as timestamptz, compute from issued_at + interval '12 months'. Month arithmetic is DST-safe in Postgres. Define "end of day" policy: expired at midnight UTC vs user's local midnight vs merchant timezone — pick one, write it down, legal may care.

Grace period question: do you want a hard cutoff or a grace window where balance shows but can't redeem? Affects UX copy and whether you need a separate expired_at vs redeemable_until.

Missing input: jurisdiction? US CARD Act bans expiry under 5 years on most retail gift cards; 12 months may be illegal depending on card type and state. Confirm legal signed off.
